panel.origin: To cook food quickly in a small amount of fat over relatively high heat, tossing or stirring frequently. A foundational fast-cooking method.
panel.mixology: She sautéed the mushrooms in butter until golden, tossing them so they browned evenly.
panel.examples: From French 'sauter' (to jump) — the food literally 'jumps' in the pan as a skilled cook flicks it, the classic toss of the sauté.

panel.origin: To cook tough cuts slowly in a covered pot with a little liquid, after first searing. Low, slow, moist heat that turns tough meat tender.
panel.mixology: He braised the short ribs for three hours until they fell apart at the touch of a fork.
panel.examples: From French 'braiser', linked to 'braise' (hot coals) — the dish was traditionally cooked over and under embers in a covered pot.

panel.origin: To briefly boil food then plunge it into ice water, stopping the cooking. Sets color, loosens skins, and softens slightly.
panel.mixology: She blanched the green beans for a minute, then shocked them in ice water to keep them vivid green.
panel.examples: From French 'blanchir' (to whiten) — originally used to whiten or pre-cook foods; the quick boil-and-shock preserves color and texture.

panel.origin: To add liquid (wine, stock) to a hot pan to dissolve the browned bits stuck to the bottom, turning them into the base of a sauce.
panel.mixology: After searing the steak, he deglazed the pan with red wine, scraping up the flavorful fond.
panel.examples: From French 'déglacer' (to unfreeze, melt). The 'glaze' of caramelized juices is dissolved back into liquid — pure concentrated flavor.

panel.origin: To combine two liquids that don't normally mix (like oil and vinegar) into a smooth, stable blend. The science behind mayonnaise and vinaigrette.
panel.mixology: She slowly whisked oil into the egg yolk to emulsify it into a thick, glossy mayonnaise.
panel.examples: From Latin 'emulgere' (to milk out) — an emulsion suspends tiny droplets of one liquid throughout another, like the fat in milk.

panel.origin: To simmer a liquid so water evaporates, concentrating its flavor and thickening it. Patience turns thin sauce into intense glaze.
panel.mixology: He let the sauce reduce by half until it coated the back of a spoon and the flavor deepened.
panel.examples: From 'reduce' in its sense of making smaller — the volume shrinks as water boils off, leaving a more concentrated, flavorful result.

panel.origin: To brown the surface of food quickly at high heat, building a flavorful crust through caramelization. The first step of countless dishes.
panel.mixology: She seared the scallops hard for ninety seconds a side, building a deep golden crust.
panel.examples: From Old English 'sēar' (dry, withered) — high heat dries and browns the surface. The crust's flavor comes from the Maillard reaction.

panel.origin: To cook food gently in liquid kept just below a simmer. The delicate heat suits eggs, fish, and fruit without toughening them.
panel.mixology: He poached the eggs in barely-trembling water until the whites set around still-runny yolks.
panel.examples: From Old French 'pochier' (to pocket) — said to describe how a poached egg's white forms a 'pocket' around the yolk.

panel.origin: To chop food into very small, fine pieces. The finest standard chop, used for garlic, herbs, and aromatics.
panel.mixology: She minced the garlic so finely it nearly melted into the sauce.
panel.examples: From Old French 'mincier' (to cut small), from Latin 'minutia' (smallness) — the same root behind 'minute' and 'diminish'.

panel.origin: To cut food into long, thin matchstick strips. A classic knife cut for vegetables, prized for its uniform, elegant shape.
panel.mixology: He julienned the carrots into delicate matchsticks for the stir-fry.
panel.examples: A French term, possibly from a chef or cook named Jean Julien; it has denoted thin matchstick cuts in cookbooks since the 18th century.

panel.origin: To cut food into tiny, precise cubes (about 1–3mm), usually by julienning first then dicing. The finest classic dice.
panel.mixology: The brunoise of peppers added a confetti of perfectly even, tiny cubes to the garnish.
panel.examples: From French, derived from Brunoy, a town near Paris. One of the most exacting cuts in classical knife work — a test of precision.

panel.origin: To slice leafy herbs or greens into thin ribbons, by stacking, rolling, and cutting. A delicate cut for basil, mint, or greens.
panel.mixology: She rolled the basil leaves and sliced a fine chiffonade to scatter over the dish.
panel.examples: From French 'chiffon' (rag, ribbon) — the technique produces thin, ribbon-like strips resembling soft fabric shreds.

panel.origin: To cut food into uniform cube shapes. Sizes range from fine to large, and evenness ensures even cooking.
panel.mixology: He diced the onion into neat, even cubes so it would cook uniformly.
panel.examples: From Old French 'des' (plural of 'de', a die for gaming) — the cubes resemble the six-sided dice used in games.

panel.origin: 'Mise en place' — having all ingredients prepped, measured, and arranged before you start cooking. The discipline that makes service run smoothly.
panel.mixology: Her mise en place was immaculate: every ingredient chopped and bowled before the first pan hit the heat.
panel.examples: French for 'putting in place'. The professional kitchen's golden rule — preparation before action — credited with separating calm cooks from chaotic ones.

panel.origin: To cut the bones out of fish or meat, producing a boneless piece. Also the boneless cut itself.
panel.mixology: The chef filleted the whole fish in seconds, sliding the knife cleanly along the bones.
panel.examples: From Old French 'filet' (a thread, little band) — the boneless strip of flesh. Pronounced 'fih-LAY' for the French cut, 'FILL-it' in British usage.

panel.origin: To cook food, usually meat or vegetables, with dry heat in an oven, browning the exterior while cooking through. A cornerstone method.
panel.mixology: She roasted the chicken at high heat until the skin turned crackling and golden.
panel.examples: From Old French 'rostir' — originally cooking on a spit over fire, now generally dry-heat oven cooking. The browning builds deep, savory flavor.

panel.origin: 'Grilling' cooks over direct heat from below; 'broiling' cooks under direct heat from above. Both use intense, direct heat to char and brown.
panel.mixology: He grilled the steaks over the coals, then finished the cheese topping under the broiler.
panel.examples: 'Grill' from Old French 'graille' (a grate); 'broil' from Old French 'bruler' (to burn) — both capture cooking by fierce, direct radiant heat.

panel.origin: To cook food with the vapor from boiling water, keeping it moist and preserving nutrients and color. A gentle, fat-free method.
panel.mixology: She steamed the dumplings over simmering water until the wrappers turned translucent.
panel.examples: From Old English 'stēam' (vapor) — the food never touches the water, cooking purely in the rising hot vapor. Prized for keeping vegetables vivid.

panel.origin: 'Deep frying' submerges food in hot oil; 'shallow frying' uses a thin layer. High heat crisps the exterior fast.
panel.mixology: They deep-fried the chicken until shatteringly crisp, then shallow-fried the next batch in less oil.
panel.examples: From Old French 'frire', from Latin 'frigere' (to roast, fry). The crisp, golden crust comes from rapid moisture loss and browning in hot fat.

panel.origin: To cook food sealed in a bag, submerged in precisely temperature-controlled water for a long time. Delivers perfect, edge-to-edge doneness.
panel.mixology: He cooked the steak sous vide at 130°F for two hours, then seared it for a perfect medium-rare throughout.
panel.examples: French for 'under vacuum' — the food is vacuum-sealed and cooked in a precise water bath. A modern technique embraced by both pros and home cooks.

panel.origin: To cook sugars (in sugar itself or in foods like onions) until they brown and develop deep, sweet, complex flavor.
panel.mixology: She caramelized the onions low and slow for an hour until they turned jammy and golden brown.
panel.examples: From 'caramel', via Spanish/Portuguese, possibly from Latin 'calamus' (reed/sugar cane). The browning of sugar at high heat creates hundreds of new flavor compounds.

panel.origin: To slowly melt the fat out of meat (like bacon or duck) over gentle heat, leaving crisp solids and usable liquid fat.
panel.mixology: He rendered the bacon slowly so the fat melted out and the strips crisped without burning.
panel.examples: From Old French 'rendre' (to give back, yield) — the heat coaxes the meat to 'yield up' its fat, separating liquid from solid.

panel.origin: A cooked paste of equal parts flour and fat, used to thicken sauces and soups. The base of countless classic dishes.
panel.mixology: She whisked a blond roux of butter and flour as the foundation for the creamy sauce.
panel.examples: French for 'reddish-brown', describing the color it takes as it cooks. Longer cooking deepens it from white to blond to brown, each with a distinct flavor.

panel.origin: A flavor base of diced onion, carrot, and celery, slowly cooked to build depth in soups, stocks, and braises. The aromatic foundation of French cooking.
panel.mixology: Every braise started with a mirepoix sweating gently in the pot to build a savory foundation.
panel.examples: Named after an 18th-century French duke, the Duc de Mirepoix, whose cook is credited with popularizing the aromatic trio.

panel.origin: A flavorful liquid made by simmering bones, vegetables, and aromatics. The backbone of soups, sauces, and braises — distinct from quicker broth.
panel.mixology: He simmered the roasted bones for hours to coax out a rich, gelatinous stock.
panel.examples: From the sense of stock as a stored supply or base. A good kitchen keeps stock on hand as the foundation that flavors everything built upon it.

panel.origin: The five classic French 'mother sauces' (Béchamel, Velouté, Espagnole, Hollandaise, Tomato) from which countless other sauces are derived.
panel.mixology: Once she mastered the five mother sauces, dozens of derivative sauces became simple variations.
panel.examples: Codified by chef Auguste Escoffier in the early 1900s; they're 'mothers' because every other classic sauce is a 'daughter' built from one of them.

panel.origin: A bundle of herbs (often parsley, thyme, bay) tied together and simmered in a dish to add flavor, then removed before serving.
panel.mixology: She dropped a bouquet garni into the simmering stock, fishing it out once it had given up its aroma.
panel.examples: French for 'garnished bouquet' — the herbs are bundled like a small bouquet so they flavor the pot, then lift out cleanly in one piece.

panel.origin: Pasta (or vegetables) cooked until tender but still firm to the bite, not soft. The proper, traditional doneness for pasta.
panel.mixology: He drained the pasta a minute early, leaving it al dente with a pleasant bite.
panel.examples: Italian for 'to the tooth' — the pasta offers a slight resistance 'to the tooth', the hallmark of properly cooked pasta in Italian tradition.

panel.origin: The colored outer peel of citrus, grated or peeled to add bright, aromatic flavor without the bitter white pith beneath.
panel.mixology: She grated lemon zest over the dish, releasing a burst of fresh citrus aroma.
panel.examples: From the original sense of 'zest' as orange or lemon peel used for flavoring — which gave rise to the figurative 'zest' meaning lively enthusiasm.

panel.origin: To '86' something means it's run out, removed from the menu, or to get rid of it. Universal kitchen shorthand for 'we're out' or 'cancel that.'
panel.mixology: '86 the salmon!' the chef called when the last fillet went out the door.
panel.examples: American diner slang of uncertain origin from the 1930s; among many theories, it may be rhyming slang for 'nix'. It spread far beyond kitchens to mean 'get rid of'.

panel.origin: To 'fire' a dish means to start cooking it now. The command that launches a plate or course toward the table.
panel.mixology: 'Fire table six!' meant the entrées for that table needed to start cooking immediately.
panel.examples: From the sense of igniting or launching into action — 'firing' a dish kicks off its cooking, timed so courses reach the table together.

panel.origin: A dish needed 'on the fly' must be made immediately and fast, usually because something went wrong or a table is waiting. Urgent, rush cooking.
panel.mixology: A plate came back wrong, so the chef had to remake it on the fly while the table waited.
panel.examples: From the sense of doing something hurriedly 'while in flight' — without the normal lead time, improvised and rushed under pressure.

panel.origin: To be 'in the weeds' is to be hopelessly overwhelmed with more orders than you can handle. The dreaded state of falling behind during service.
panel.mixology: Three big tables sat at once and suddenly the whole line was in the weeds.
panel.examples: A vivid metaphor of being lost and tangled in tall weeds, unable to find your way out — kitchen and restaurant slang for drowning in work.

panel.origin: 'All day' is the running total of how many of an item are needed across all current orders. 'Three burgers all day' means three total, right now.
panel.mixology: 'That's six steaks all day!' the expediter called, tallying every table's order into one number.
panel.examples: Kitchen shorthand for the grand total 'in all' — it cuts through ticket-by-ticket confusion so the line knows the full count at a glance.

panel.origin: Restaurant shorthand for a table that seats a given number — a 'two-top' seats two, a 'four-top' seats four. Basic front-and-back-of-house lingo.
panel.mixology: The host seated a two-top by the window and a six-top in the corner booth.
panel.examples: From 'top' meaning a tabletop, with the number indicating its seats — efficient shorthand for communicating table size across a busy restaurant.
